desc:
The spectacle that is the confluence of the rushing rivers to the south and east
can be best viewed here by standing close to a twisted iron railing. The white
foam created by the meeting of the turbulent waters looks almost like a sea of
clouds resting upon the waters. Much of the rail hangs down over the edge,
teasing the swirling confluence of rivers below.
name: Riverbluff Walk

desc:
This narrow walk travels along the outer rim of the sunken hillside. Carved high
into the granite bluff here it meets a small footpath leading north. A mangled
wrought iron railing coloured so dark it cannot be seen at night lines the
southern edge of the walk. Perhaps the residents of this city did not walk this
path after sunset or perhaps they had walked so many times admiring the rivers
beauty they needed no rail to warn them of the bluff's edge.
name: Riverbluff Walk

desc:
Specks of dirt and granite flakes, from the path to the north, have drifted down
the hillside and cover the northern side of this narrow walkway. A large tree
has fallen diagonally across the walkway and continues on to hang over the cliff
to the south. Tangled in the branches are the remains of a wrought iron railing
that once warned weary travellers of the abrupt edge. From its size the tree
appears to be at least a thousand years old. An odd place for a tree to have
grown so close, this one obviously took root and died all in the interim of time
passed since this city's downfall.
name: Riverbluff Walk

desc:
A wrought iron railing to the southwest and this once smooth walkway is the only
evidence that this land was ever inhabited. This part of the walk is badly
overgrown. The wild bushes block most of the walk and have entangled themselves
in and around the railing obscuring it. The walkway continues up some overgrown
and mildewed stone steps to the north and continues on eastward perhaps to
better preserved areas of this kingdom.
name: Riverbluff Walk

desc:
Various fungi and spores flourish here covering every inch of the decaying
foundation. Metal spools indicate that this might once have been a ropeworks.
Being so close to the river it is quite possible this was a place to purchase
and repair fishing nets. Perhaps the algae on nets that long ago dissolved into
nothingness is what gave birth to the rampant mould.
name: Decaying Ropeworks

desc:
Perhaps this was once a beautiful, low-arched granite causeway which spanned the
northern edge of the harbour down below. Through the ages, however, time has
erased all of the grandeur it once held. Several thick stone support beams jut
out irregularly to the side causing the bridge to lean precariously sideways,
reminding the cautious traveller that too much weight here would not be prudent.
name: A Skewed Stone Causeway

desc:
Carved into the riverside bluff, this narrow path is bordered on the north by
hillside and south by the rushing river. Even after centuries of abandonment,
the path is still visible through the mass of weeds and clumps of dirt that have
fallen from higher ground. From this point one cannot help but hear the rumbling
river flow.
name: Riverbluff Walk

desc:
Metal rods welded together to form the frame of a boat lay here, untouched for
ages on the exposed foundation of this structure. Perhaps long ago a shipwright
carefully built this boat here in his shop. He may have sat carefully fitting
pieces of wood into the frame forming the curved base of a small sailing vessel.
Wound on a metal stand are several lengths of chain accompanied by several
anchors of varying sizes. Most of the metal chain has turned a bright, rusted
amber colour.
name: Perishing Depot

desc:
As you step off the edge of the tower ruins you feel a momentary euphoria
comparable to flying before you plummet downward.
name: Airy Descent

desc:
Not only has the roof been sheared off the top of this tower, but two of this
upper chamber's walls are completely gone as well. The remaining walls, south
and west, are not much more than piles of cracked and crumbling granite bricks.
Even after centuries of decay scoring on the bricks is a monument to the weapons
of destruction launched against this once fair city. One can imagine the high
elves standing before the now severed rectangular windows observing the day to
day activities all over the city.
name: Sheared Observatory

desc:
As you step off the edge of the tower ruins you feel a momentary euphoria
comparable to flying before you plummet downward.
name: Airy Descent

desc:
A large oval-shaped table made of granite has fallen off two of its four legs
and splintered a portion of the floor. Piles of dust surrounding the table look
to be the remnants of many wooden chairs broken down by the elements. Two large
silver hooks are embedded into the northern wall, much like the sets of smaller
hooks above windows throughout this tower. However here no windows exist. It is
quite possible these hooks once held up a grand tapestry.
name: Forsaken Council Chamber

desc:
Worse to the west than in other directions, the plaza tiles are badly damaged.
While age has clearly taken its toll on these exposed slabs of granite most of
this damage seems to be the ravages of war. The facade of the shop to the west
has been breached in several places as if the small shop took the more of the
attackers energy than the rest of this area.
name: Plaza Engulfed in Ruin

desc:
Even in its ruinous condition the once four-story citadel casts a wide shadow
over the plaza below. Only in the very early hours of the morning does sunlight
shine on this eastern side of the great tower unobstructed. By noon the lack of
light hides the vast damage to the two-tone granite tiles that make up the plaza
grounds. To the west is a great doorway made wider by what must have been some
uninvited guests using powerful weaponry.
name: In the Shadow of the Citadel

desc:
Thick granite tiles, a mix of light and dark, lying shattered on the ground were
once a part of this plaza ground. So severely damaged are the once smooth
polished tiles that they are now little more than flakes of rock holding
tenuously on to their position as part of a grand checkerboard design. The
patterned tiles end at the doorways of several crumbling structures. Bricks made
of a similar granite, fallen over centuries from the citadel above, lay
scattered about.
name: A Shattered Plaza

desc:
Iron rods twisted into floral designs form at least twenty tables and numerous
chairs that are scattered all over this enclosure. It is clear they were
carefully constructed for outdoor use. There is no sign they were ever adorned
by fabric or upholstery yet they look to have been quite comfortable. Part of
what seems to have been an outdoor cafe, these ancient furnishings have
withstood the centuries. A wall half as high as the ones that form this room
obscure what is left of a stone stairway leading downward.
name: Desolate Eatery

desc:
Almost two stories high, this single room must have once welcomed many honoured
guests to this centre of elven culture, and some very unwelcome ones. The
meticulously etched silver doors lie far against the western wall, severely
battered. The doors were hit so hard that several feet of the surrounding wall
was knocked in with them. A chandelier of hand blown glass has long ago fallen
from its high perch and shattered in a million tiny shards all over the floor. A
winding stair attached to the southern wall leads up toward this structure's
crest.
name: Venerable Rotunda

desc:
A single stone counter lays cracked here upon the floor amidst layer upon layer
of the dusty remains of wood and shattered glass cases. A variety of small
ornate stone boxes together with the shattered glass showcases give evidence
that this was a jewellery shop. This place seems to have been violently torn
apart. Every brick making up the walls has been punctured or cracked in the
middle, as if someone was looking for a hidden compartment or chamber. Even the
bricks in the floor have been searched. With the amount of force used to break
these stones it's amazing that the floor has not collapsed. The walls have not
withstood the destruction and ages so well; to the north and south they only
stand half their original height.
name: A Dismantled Shop

desc:
A crack in the ground emanating from the east ends here in a sunken crater
several feet wide. Many of the alternating dark and light coloured granite tiles
that make up this plaza's surface have over the centuries fallen into this hole.
Scoring on the surrounding stone and along the crack gives evidence of a great
impact of a deliberate nature.
name: Perishing Plaza

desc:
What looks to be the top of the citadel above has found a new home after
crashing through the ceiling of this small shop. Only two walls of the shop
remain even partially intact. This portion of stone tower that fell from the sky
was so heavy that not only did it destroy everything left inside here but it
also pierced the floor leading to the room below. There is no way to discern
what type of wares this place once sold. Judging by the amount of shattered
glass that litters the ground, whatever it was must have been in liquid form.
name: Resting Place of the Toppled Tower

desc:
Covered in the debris of falling bricks from the citadel above one can barely
discern the alternating light and dark granite tile design that cover this
ancient plaza. Whole sections of the tower above in the form of crumbling stone
fell upon this area with such great force they are embedded a foot into the
ground. Tiny flakes of granite float up in the air on the occasional gusts of
wind.
name: Amidst the Broken Tiles

desc:
Three vertical bookcases containing a vast amount of both horizontal and
vertical storage areas stand here, looking as sturdy as the granite walls that
form this room. Etched out of granite themselves, with decorative accents around
each section, they were designed to showcase, store and protect the manuscripts,
tomes, and sketches that described the art of the elven smiths. Whereas at one
time each leaf of paper must have been carefully filed, its proper place
annotated by small letters carved into the bookcase, now all is a shambles.
Shreds of paper line the floor and not a single book or scroll remains on the
ornate shelves.
name: Master Repository

desc:
This hallway's etched columns, like the arched entranceway to this wing,
showcases the grandeur and elegance of this place crafted by its elven
inhabitants. More artful than functional, this large hall's only purpose is to
provide entry to the northern vault and the library to the south. Every detail
from the carved mouldings that line the floor to the stone picture frames carved
into the walls shows how important it was for the Gwaith-i-Mirdain to spare no
effort and settle for nothing less than perfection in their crafts.
name: The East Hall

desc:
Steel shelves of varying depths protrude from the north and east walls. The west
wall is lined with hooks of adequate size to hold metal shields. Looking closer
at the shelves one can see threads of material, seeming to have been torn from
silken cloths. Under foot are the bashed and battered remains of what might have
once been considered an impenetrable metal cabinet's doors. Some great force
seems to have sent them sprawling giving the intruders access to the many small
sections of this vault. They stole whatever was contained ripping the silken
cloths that covered the smaller swords and ornaments.
name: Inside an Engraved Vault

desc:
A meticulously carved granite arch forms the entrance to this wing of the
underground halls. Higher than could be reached by two elves, one standing on
the other's shoulders, it has remained undisturbed since its construction. This
arch is supported by the same squared granite spires that support the domed
ceiling. It must have taken careful work to carve such a ceiling out of the
solid granite bluff while preserving the strength needed for the bluff to
support the city above. Everything about this place is a work of art. Those that
built these halls must have taken great pride in each detail.
name: Arch of the Jewelsmiths

desc:
Inside an arched granite frame sit two formidable tall bronze doors. Left
unpolished for centuries the metal is discoloured in a few places. While the
deep carvings that accent these doors draw some attention, far more noticeable
here is the lack of a door handle of any sort. In the place of a handle, on the
right door, seems to be an oval shaped recess with some elven runes carved into
the door around the opening. Turning away from the doors one can see the large
columns that run the course of this great hall and hear the rush of water from
the water wall to the northwest.
name: Before the Bronze Doors

desc:
Along the smooth granite wall to the south are three large statues of Noldor
elves. Equally spaced from one another they are all similar in size and none is
given more importance than the others are. The carefully carved features
showcase the work of a master sculptor. Each figure is carved in full attire
with sheathed sword and crested shield lying at their feet. The writings etched
in pedestals, apparently the names of these three, read Finwe, Ingwe and Elwe.
Some smaller writing appears on the eastern wall.
name: In the Presence of the Eldar

desc:
Slight splashes of water from the harnessed geyser to the west have moistened
the floor. The cascade of white water rises with great force, hitting the domed
ceiling above and falling back down upon its source. It forms a wall of water
that is quite a marvel to behold. The elves through a feat of engineering have
manage to harness this underground water source as it shot up toward the surface
and redirected it so it not only could not continue its ascent but also was
aesthetically pleasing to them.
name: Ledge along a Water Wall

desc:
Like its counterpart corner to the west, this partial granite column fills the
gap where the north and east walls would meet. Its smooth round surface seems to
have been polished to a flawless shine. The column portion rests upon an etched
pedestal that along with the walls supports its weight. About eye level up the
column is a floral etching that when examined closely appears to in fact be some
elven runes written in a way as to conceal themselves. A light mist of water
fills this area, emanating from the fountain water wall nearby.
name: A Carved Corner

desc:
The granite tile floor, once polished to a perfect reflective surface so that
not a single pore remained to collect the water here, is not well preserved.
Looking up one can see a small crack in the dome above used to channel the water
downward. So precise is this water design that the small crack has misguided the
flow and allowed some of the water to fall on the floor here wearing it down
over centuries of abandonment and neglect.
name: Concealed Precipice

desc:
A tremendous thrust of water current propels you up into the domed granite
ceiling with such force that it shatters every bone in your body.
name: Captured Geyser

desc:
One quarter of a carved column sticks out in place of what would be the sharp
edge of two walls meeting. A meticulously etched pedestal at the base of the
platform along with its position in the corner space supports the solid granite
column's weight. Some markings carved at eye level seem to be runes but the
etchings are so fanciful these could easily be mistaken for decorative
flourishes.
name: A Carved Corner

desc:
A light spray of water from the wall of water to the east keeps this narrow
ledge moist. The vertical stream shoots up from a forceful geyser below, crashes
into the domed roof above and cascades back down upon its source. Other than the
misty spray, the precise curvature of the roof keeps the surrounding areas dry.
Looking downward over the edge of this shelf, one can see nothing but foamy
white water.
name: Ledge along a Water Wall

desc:
Two tall stately silvery doors sit inside an arched frame in the western wall. A
bit tarnished with age, the base of the doors shows some rust from the moisture
in the air and a lack of polishing. Even more noticeable than the carvings in
the thick doors is the fact they are missing any semblance of handles. In the
place of a handle, on the left door, seems to be an oval shaped recess with some
elven runes carved into the door around the opening. Turning away from the doors
one can see the large columns that run the course of this great hall and hear
the rush of water from the water wall to the northeast.
name: Before the Silvery Doors

desc:
The sight to the north dominates this entire room. What appears to be a wall
made of water blocks passage northward. Looking down you see the water shoots up
through the floor and with an enormous thrust hits the domed ceiling above. The
curvature of the ceiling then forces the water back downward in a mass of
cascading waterfalls. Some water has escaped this feat of elven engineering and
lays still upon the floor.
name: Concourse Amidst the Spray

desc:
This is the entrance point into the grand underground hall. The hall itself is
as extraordinarily ornate as it is enormous. Columns more than three elves tall
and about half as wide give support to the ceiling. They form two horizontal
rows dividing this vast hall into several sections. Where the arched ceiling
meets the smooth granite columns are elaborate etchings, some with carefully
applied gold leaf laid so thin it cannot be scraped off. Each column is adorned
with a decorative vine pattern in a serpentine arrangement from top to base. The
hall, complete with its own large mural size etched patterns, stretches out in
all directions.
name: Hall of the Mirdain

desc:
The eight greatest of the Valar are represented here in statue, four along the
southern wall and three along the western and one in the corner. In order stand
Manwe, Varda, Ulmo, Yavanna, Aule, Mandos, Nienna and Orome. Though Manwe was
held to be the High King of Arda his statue is no larger than that of the
others, showing all eight were held in equal reverence. However, the statue of
Aule stands at an angle in the corner with stone benches at either side. Perhaps
this was done because Aule is the Vala concerned with the substance of Arda,
rocks, metals, and minerals, endearing him to the elven smiths.
name: In the Presence of the Valar

desc:
Starting with a single narrow step, this stairway increases in width as it
sweeps down gently northward to the hall below. Above the top step, engraved in
the granite archway, are some elven runes etched so smooth each letter appears
to flow into the next. The steps, so well constructed they still look polished,
have well stood the ages. This place is, however, not without its scars. Sharp
cuts in the steps and slices in the walls give evidence a great duel was fought.
No doubt these had to have been mythical swords to be able to penetrate the
stone in such a fashion. The fine silver banister, running the length of the
stairs on both sides, is misshapen in a way that suggests a mighty warrior may
have fallen upon it.
name: Threshold to the House of Mirdain

desc:
Several broken stone bookcases lie on their sides having been knocked down
against the west wall. Elven words carved in some rounded stone posts that once
stood at the end of each bookshelf seem to denote an ancient filing system.
Lying on the floor are the remains of several stone boxes about the size of a
large journal. It would have taken a rather strong person to have lifted these
heavy stone containers high enough to have cracked them open against the floor
exposing the manuscripts inside. Brown flaky dust covers the floor along with
some small brass fasteners, apparently all that is left of this library's
contents, eroded into nothingness over the course of centuries.
name: Toppled Stacks

desc:
Broad black smears cross the barren walls of this once great vaulted chamber of
antiquities. When the sun or moon is aglow, bars of light cast through rents in
the walls making motes of dust glitter throughout. Scorch-marks mar the walls,
the floor, the ceiling. Shattered display cases of iron and glass litter the
hall. It appears that whatever great heirlooms may have been housed here were
looted long ago. A formidable stone staircase angles both upwards to the room
above and twists downward to the room below.
name: Raided Lower Gallery

desc:
This hall with its high ceiling and ornate granite arches serves little function
other than allowing entrance into the chamber to the west. A small granite
bench, perhaps long enough to seat two elves, is pushed against the eastern
wall. One could assume that those having business with or being called before
the Gwaith-i-Mirdain were often kept waiting in this place. To the east are two
extremely tall silver doors. Large murals cover the north and south walls.
name: An Ornate Archway

desc:
This square chamber lies between two sets of grand doors at the north and south.
The granite walls stand like curtains between sets of large carved columns. The
wall is constructed so it appears to be dividing the columns so only half of
each can be seen within this room. The high domed ceiling carved out of the
bluff above contains a beautiful painted mural that even time and the
condensation of this moist underground has not damaged.
name: Inside a Circular Antechamber

desc:
Five large, elegant chairs of stone adorned with silver and gold are arranged in
a semi-circle along the northern wall. Large hand blown glass chandeliers hang
down in two long columns running north to south along the granite ceiling. The
luminescence, though dimmed with age, is still rather brilliant leading one to
believe they burn with a magical fire. A royal blue carpet runs the length of
the chamber, from the entrance all the way up to the throne chairs. Tattered and
frayed, it appears to have once cushioned visitors' knees as they paid homage to
the elven smiths. The walls are adorned with many finely engraved murals and
designs depicting the history of the Noldor.
name: Court of the Mirdain

desc:
A long rectangular stone table takes up much of the area of this small room. A
large hand-blown glass chandelier casts a pattern of soft circular lights on the
tabletop. Five carved stone chairs are carefully tucked under each side of the
table ready to seat the elves long ago gone from this place. A tall chair at the
southern end of the table commands a great presence over the rest of the table.
Unlike the other rooms in these halls, the walls here are barren, giving
evidence this was a place for serious discussions and distractions, even those
of the simplest kind, were unwarranted. To the north lie two large metal doors;
when closed their smooth surfaces line up perfectly with this chambers walls.
name: Councillors Chamber

desc:
This spiral staircase made of iron is so well constructed that even under the
weight of those using it, it makes no creaking sounds. The forty steps follow a
precise and equidistant cascading arrangement. A slightly tarnished silver
handrail, made from one piece of precisely curved metal, follows the steps from
top to bottom. This stairway was clearly designed to take up very little room,
entirely contained in a five-foot wide cylindrical space. This hides it well
behind the wall of water a bit to the south. From the other side of the water
the staircase is completely concealed. Several people could use these steps at
the same time single file in the same direction, however passage up and down
simultaneously would be impossible.
name: Top of the Serpentine Stairs

desc:
From this short curved hall one can see the spiral staircase to the west and the
wall of water just inside the room to the south. Along the carved granite walls
are several square shaped insets about a foot deep. Inside one of the showcases
are hooks and fasteners about the right size to have held swords and knives on
display. Another looks as if a shield could have been displayed in its recess.
The third inset is much smaller then the other two, seemingly to display
jewellery, judging by its size and some narrow platforms set within.
name: Inner Aperture

desc:
This spacious, carved granite workshop once housed all that was necessary to
take raw materials and craft them into works of art. A small, extinguished forge
extrudes from the west wall. Row upon row of miniature pegs line the sides of
the room securing detailing tools needed for the task of smithing. Wooden
workbenches lie splintered about in ruins. The great treasures that were
undoubtedly housed here vanished long ago, still there might be some small items
that were overlooked.
name: Artisan Works

desc:
The air is humid in this place far below the great elven city. The only light
sources are the dim blue flames from tiny iron lanterns that hang just below
where the west wall meets the domed ceiling. The eastern edge of this wide
granite platform shows a bit of wear from the rush of the natural water
forcefully redirected through this channel of elven make. Carved out of the
western wall is an elegantly arched doorway. Deeply etched runes provide a
decorative border around the frame of this doorway.
name: Candescence Hall

desc:
This delicate looking bridge is a work of art as well as function. Suspended
above the watery channel it hangs from several woven chains. Each piece
consisting of six finely crafted silver chains around an iron support rod. The
walkway of the bridge is made of slats of heavy iron ore with fine silver folded
over them several times. The entire presentation makes the bridge appear
entirely made of silver. Adding to the illusion is the silvery reflection it
casts on the water below. The fast flowing current stirs up a gentle breeze
heading southward.
name: Gilded Suspension Bridge

desc:
From this dimly lit hall one can see straight across the elven-made channel to
the west. The fast flowing water shoots through the circular conduit with such
intensity that the western edge of this platform's granite floor has been
forever smoothed by erosion. One of the tiles has broken loose, giving evidence
that through neglect even the most well made of structures can fall prey to the
elements. Two of the small iron lamps that run the course of the eastern wall
are lacking their magical blue flames. A doorway carved out of the eastern wall
is replete with runes surrounded by a decorative design.
name: Coruscate Hall

desc:
The absence of tools and artifacts from this workshop might lead one to believe
that any wealth once contained here has now departed. Whoever looted this shop
seems to have been exceedingly interested in the detailing of gemstones. Several
fractured bookshelves lie irreparable on the ground, stripped of any books they
may have contained. Perhaps upon close examination some small setting of gold or
silver might be found.
name: Gemstone Works

desc:
There are several directions to chose from at this junction in the river bluff
walk. The walk curves a bit to the north, aiming travellers toward a decaying
structure. To the east one can ascend an aged causeway over the bluff. The sound
of river water beckons to the south and this scenic walk continues upward. A
plethora of choices in which one can walk in the long ago forgotten footsteps of
this once grand city's residents. There is a sticky substance covering the
ground to the north.
name: Riverbluff Walk

desc:
Five rectangular stone pillars coming out of the water look to be part of an
ancient pier. A sixth pillar must have existed at some point but it is gone now,
either crumbled or washed away. It is easy to imagine planks of wood running
between these stones creating an S-shape landing stretching out over the river.
name: Receding Pier

desc:
A long metal chain with links so strong they have withstood the centuries
extends from a stone column into the depths of the river. Several other stone
columns extend out in a line several feet into the river. The various water
stages of the river have left their marks on the columns by eroding and
discolouring the granite. One can imagine that in the past the citizens of this
ancient city docked their ships in this safe river harbour.
name: Empty Harbour

desc:
A few stone posts and some badly rusted chains are all that is left of this
abandoned pier. So old is the chain, one good tug from a small child would
surely loosen several links. Much of the bluff has eroded over the centuries
leaving some portions totally surrounded by water. Each time the river rises
much of the southern section of this area floods leaving everything here
knee-deep under water.
name: Flooded Pier
